bibliographic.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French), Part I, The Text, Early English Text Society Extra Series 67 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1895), vii + 216 pp.
bibliographic.publisher: Published for the Early English Text Society by Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Extra Series 67
bibliographic.title: The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French), Part I, The Text

entities.description: The complete first printed edition of the Middle English romance, with Furnivall's forewords, the narrative on pp. 1–207, a note on p. 208, and Thomas Austin's glossary on pp. 209–216. The projected Part II was to contain Leon Kellner's introduction and language/source study but was never published.
entities.label: F. J. Furnivall, The Three Kings' Sons (1895)
notes: The title page's unique-manuscript claim is correct only for the surviving Middle English tradition; the French source work has multiple witnesses.
Furnivall's about-1500 witness date is superseded for preferred current description by the British Library's c. 1475–c. 1485 range.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1895. U-M currently labels the represented materials public domain and No Copyright–United States, while U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A states that works published before 1 January 1931 are in the public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1895 transcription, forewords, editorial glosses and notes, and Austin's glossary
notes: This United States determination does not by itself resolve provider terms or every other jurisdiction.

entities.description: An unpublished working copy of the English text made for the Early English Text Society. Furnivall calls it an excellent copy and says he took up the edition after Leon Kellner could not do so.
entities.label: Mrs Leon Kellner's working transcript of Harley MS 326
notes: Furnivall does not state Mrs Kellner's personal name, the transcript's repository, or its later survival.

entities.description: A documented but unpublished comparison of the English text with what Furnivall calls its French original in Paris, prepared for the projected Part II.
entities.label: Leon Kellner's unpublished English-French collation
notes: No surviving notes, published Part II, or evidence that the collation altered Part I was found.

entities.description: The physical comparison object behind Kellner's collation. Furnivall does not supply a shelfmark, repository, format, or recension; the neutral repository-artifact type avoids falsely choosing one surviving French manuscript.
entities.label: Unidentified Paris copy of Les trois fils de rois used by Leon Kellner
notes: This object is not identified with BnF français 92, the former No. 6766 discussed by Ward, or any other known French witness.
It is also not asserted to be the medieval English translator's physical exemplar.

access.accessMethod: The current catalog exposes a legacy full-display link but explicitly labels the digital images currently unavailable
notes: This dated negative finding is not evidence that the manuscript was never digitized.
The catalog also says selected coverage existed in the former Catalogue of Illuminated Manuscripts, but no current exact image payload could be audited.

access.accessMethod: Register or renew a Reader Pass, submit the online item request with shelfmark Harley MS 326, and consult manuscripts in London; the catalog says a letter of introduction is required

access.contact: British Library Digitisation Services; permissions@bl.uk for copying or reproduction permission
access.format: Quote-dependent reference JPEG or reproduction-quality photography
notes: Request the upper and lower boards, spine, pastedowns, gilt edges if offered, all three initial unfoliated flyleaves [i–iii], every surface of fols. 1–123 including the adjacent chronicle, all three terminal unfoliated flyleaves [124–126], and every blank or detached element.
Identify the work portion as ff. 8r–123v without cropping the requested whole codex.
Ask for color photography; BL says original manuscripts cannot be scanned and surrogate scans may be black and white unless color is requested.
Obtain written reuse terms before any public repository or print-on-demand redistribution.

rights.basis: No current exact image object was available to inspect. British Library Imaging Services says users must clear relevant third-party rights and must not copy or reproduce supplied material without written British Library permission, which may entail additional fees.

dateStatements.label: Ward's historical catalog date
dateStatements.notes: Retained as an attributed older description, not the preferred current date.

entities.description: A composite codex whose paper ff. 1r–7r contain a separate Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV and whose parchment ff. 8r–123v contain the sole known surviving Middle English manuscript of The Three Kings' Sons. The romance has forty-five unnumbered chapters indicated by illuminated initials and twenty-two miniatures.

holding.notes: All manuscripts and archives must be consulted in London; the catalog says a letter of introduction is required.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 326
notes: Unique means the sole known surviving Middle English manuscript witness, not the only material witness to the French source romance.
The four much-later-hand English lines above the Latin colophon are annotation, not part of the medieval translation.

physicalDescription.extent: 123 folios plus six unfoliated parchment flyleaves: three before the foliation and three after it
physicalDescription.format: Quarto codex in post-1600 brown leather binding
notes: The target romance occupies parchment ff. 8r–123v; the paper chronicle at ff. 1r–7r is outside the work.
The current catalog gives England, probably London, as witness origin and describes Gothic cursive script.
Four half-page and eighteen smaller miniatures accompany the romance.
physicalDescription.support: Paper at ff. 1–7; parchment at ff. 8–123

rights.basis: The text is attested in a manuscript dated c. 1475–c. 1485 and was published in 1895; the determination does not apply to modern photographs, metadata, or contractual access conditions.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English textual content at ff. 8r–123v, excluding modern photography and catalog data

rights.basis: No current exact image object was available to inspect. British Library Imaging Services says users must clear relevant third-party rights and must not copy or reproduce supplied material without written British Library permission, which may entail additional fees.

next_steps: Inspect Henry Grinberg's complete 1975 manuscript-filiation article and Giovanni Palumbo's 2001 edition and 2002 manuscript study.
Search EETS archival correspondence and Kellner papers for a Paris shelfmark or collation notebook.
Keep the abstract translation relation in work metadata and do not identify BnF français 92 or another extant French witness as the physical exemplar without provenance evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which French recension and physical copy was the medieval translator's exemplar, and which Paris copy did Leon Kellner collate?

next_steps: Search Early English Text Society archives, Furnivall papers, and Kellner papers for the transcript, proofs, and correspondence.
Do not add a direct Furnivall-to-Harley transcription or collation edge until an edition statement, proof record, or correspondence documents that consultation.
If the working materials survive, compare them with the printed edition to identify Furnivall's interventions.
openQuestions.question: Do Mrs Leon Kellner's transcript and Leon Kellner's French collation survive, and did Furnivall directly recheck the full text against Harley MS 326?

next_steps: Ask whether the unavailable legacy full-display object or selected illuminated-manuscript images can be restored through a current exact delivery endpoint.
If not, order the complete codex: binding, pastedowns, edges if offered, all six unfoliated flyleaves, every surface of ff. 1–123, blanks, and detached matter; map the romance ff. 8r–123v separately.
Request color photography and written permission terms for repository and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand reuse before publishing images.
After receipt, verify every chapter initial, miniature, folio boundary, and later annotation against the edition and XML.
notes: The general rate card is not a shelfmark-specific quote.

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for a written interpretation covering raw XML, close derivatives, repository redistribution, and print-on-demand outputs.
Until clarified, preserve both dated assertions and distinguish copyright status from provider terms.
Do not infer that the CME repository's software-framed Unlicense automatically resolves the older U-M restriction.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M's current Public Domain / No Copyright–United States statement supersede the embedded 1997 prohibition on redistribution by commercial and not-for-profit content providers?

next_steps: Search historical OTA catalog exports and U-M project documentation by title, UMDL identifier, edition, and source checksum.

subject.description: comparative discussion of The Three Kings' Sons

relations.subjectLabel: Ruth Helen Caddick, Clariodus study, volume 1 (2023)

reviewNotes: The lineage primary subject is the repository artifact, not the abstract romance.
The cataloged work is the complete Middle English romance at Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v; the Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV at ff. 1r–7r is adjacent codex content, not part of ThreeKSon.
Ward identifies forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters marked by illuminated initials. Furnivall's running glosses provide the displayed chapter labels, and U-M reuses those labels as DIV1 heads.
Part I is a bibliographic promise of a projected scholarly continuation, not an incomplete medieval text: the narrative reaches its explicit on printed p. 207.
The Mrs Kellner transcript and Leon Kellner French collation are modeled as distinct editorial intermediaries. No claim is made that Kellner's French collation changed the published Part I.

The physical French object used by Leon Kellner and the physical exemplar used by the medieval translator are both unidentified; neither is equated with BnF français 92 or another surviving French witness.
The current British Library c. 1475–c. 1485 date is preferred for Harley MS 326 while Ward's about-1500 description and Furnivall's title-page wording remain attributed historical assertions.

French belongs to the source-work and Kellner-comparison layers. The work text is Middle English, with a two-line Latin witness colophon; four later-hand English lines are annotation rather than an intrinsic verse section.

lineage.summary: The checked-in artifact is a pinned repository version of University of Michigan CME item ThreeKSon, a 1997 keyboarded encoding of F. J. Furnivall's 1895 EETS edition. U-M encodes the complete Middle English prose romance on printed pp. 1–207 while omitting the edition's forewords, p. 208 note, and pp. 209–216 glossary. Furnivall edited the text from Mrs Leon Kellner's working transcript of the sole surviving Middle English witness, British Library Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v; no direct Furnivall-to-manuscript collation is asserted without further evidence. Leon Kellner separately collated the English text against an unidentified French copy in Paris for a projected Part II that did not appear. The conceptual French source-work relation is recorded in work metadata because the translator's physical exemplar is unknown. A complete Robarts scan of the 1895 volume and its OCR/scandata derivatives are cached, as is relevant supporting scholarship. The British Library catalog's former whole-manuscript digital link is currently marked unavailable, so no manuscript facsimile is claimed as downloaded; a complete-codex request route is documented instead.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; French source also anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Late fifteenth century; attested by the sole surviving English witness dated c. 1475–c. 1485
catalogSummary.editor: F. J. Furnivall (1895 source edition), from a working transcript made by Mrs Leon Kellner
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of translation unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Three Kings' Sons

catalogingSubject.label: The Three Kings' Sons
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English prose translation of the anonymous French romance Les trois fils de rois, represented completely in the pinned XML. This intellectual work is distinct from its sole known surviving Middle English manuscript witness, the adjacent chronicle in Harley MS 326, Mrs Leon Kellner's working transcript, Leon Kellner's unpublished French collation, Furnivall's 1895 edition, the 1997 U-M encoding, and the checked-in repository artifact.

contentParts.label: The Three Kings' Sons
notes: The forty-five chapter boundaries are summarized rather than repeated as forty-five intrinsic titled parts because their supplied labels are Furnivall's running glosses, not manuscript rubrics.

Furnivall's label Part I is bibliographic: the complete narrative is present. The projected Part II was to contain Kellner's introduction, vocabulary, syntax, and source study, not the missing end of the romance.
contentParts.summary: The complete prose romance, from the upbringing of Philip and the appeal for aid to Sicily through the three princes' concealed service, the defeat of the Turkish forces, the Iolante tournament, royal marriages, conversions, and the closing account of the three kings' descendants.

dateStatements.display: Late fifteenth century; no later than the surviving witness dated c. 1475–c. 1485
notes: The witness supplies a terminus ante quem, not a securely localized translation event.
The older circa-1500 date belongs to Ward's witness description and is not preferred over the current British Library range.

dateStatements.display: c. 1475–c. 1485 in British Library Harley MS 326
1895 in F. J. Furnivall's EETS Extra Series 67 edition

scope.description: Digital representation date, not a date of medieval composition or translation

extent.description: One complete prose work in forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters. The XML represents those chapters as ThreeKSon:1–45, with 115 prose paragraphs outside note ancestry, 207 print-page markers for Furnivall pp. 1–207, 231 explicit folio-side milestones spanning 8v–123v after the opening f. 8r statement, twenty-four NOTE1 elements, and six L elements: two Latin colophon lines in the work text and four later-hand English lines quoted in an editorial note. Encoding counts are not a canonical word or paragraph count.

formStatements.description: One continuous prose romance organized in the witness as forty-five unnumbered chapters indicated by illuminated initials.

assertion.notes: The romance frames dynastic adventure and chivalric warfare as defense of Christian Sicily against a fictionalized Turkish power; the term describes this literary construction, not historical reportage.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The two-line Latin colophon is witness paratext; four English lines copied by a much later hand are annotation. Neither changes the primary language facet.
Furnivall's Part I contains the complete narrative; the projected Part II was a scholarly introduction and language/source study and was never published.
Harley MS 326 is the sole known surviving Middle English manuscript witness, not the sole witness to the multilingual romance: ARLIMA lists eight surviving French manuscripts.
The current British Library c. 1475–c. 1485 witness date is preferred while Ward's older about-1500 description and Furnivall's title-page wording remain attributed historical assertions.

Internet Archive metadata assigns David Aubert as a creator, but current source-work evidence treats the French romance as anonymous and Aubert as a copyist of one French witness; the provider heading is not adopted as authorship.
No Oxford Text Archive identifier or transfer statement appears in the XML header or pinned CME history; a possible OTA intermediary remains unconfirmed in the lineage questions.

next_steps: Consult Henry Grinberg's complete 1975 filiation study and subsequent scholarship on the Anglo-Burgundian transmission.
Separate translation history from Harley 326's c. 1475–c. 1485 production in England, probably London.
Do not promote the surviving witness's southern English dialect or probable London origin into a proven translation location without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: When, where, and for whom was the Middle English translation made?

next_steps: Compare Grinberg 1975 with Giovanni Palumbo's 2001 edition and 2002 manuscript study.
Trace EETS archives for Mrs Kellner's transcript, Leon Kellner's collation, and any surviving shelfmark correspondence.
Keep the conceptual translation_of relation to Les trois fils de rois separate from any surviving French witness until physical identity is evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Which French recension and physical copy supplied the English translator, and which Paris copy did Leon Kellner collate?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the unavailable legacy full-digital object can be restored.
Otherwise request the whole codex, not only ff. 8r–123v, and map each illuminated initial against the edition's forty-five headings.
Continue to label the boundaries as manuscript-supported and the displayed heading texts as editorial.
openQuestions.question: Can all forty-five manuscript chapter boundaries and their relationship to Furnivall's running glosses be checked against a complete current color facsimile?

notes: This broad English context does not turn the witness's probable London production into a proven place of translation.
Caddick describes the surviving text as southern English; that linguistic observation remains distinct from a precise translation location.
The closing formula says the book has been translated, but it does not name the translator or identify the physical French exemplar.

scope.description: Middle English translation of the anonymous French prose romance

notes: Editorial responsibility is a transmission-layer fact and does not imply intellectual authorship or translation.

scope.description: The complete Middle English text in the 1895 source edition

notes: The transcript's present location and survival are unknown.

scope.description: Working copy of the English text prepared for the Early English Text Society

assertion.notes: Furnivall states that the collation had been made for a projected Part II; he does not say that it altered the published Part I text.

scope.description: Comparison of the English text with an unidentified French copy in Paris

subjects.label: Christian-Muslim conflict and conversion
notes: The text's representations of Turks, Islam, baptism, and conversion are late-medieval Christian literary constructions and should not be read as neutral ethnography.

subjects.label: Chivalry, tournaments, warfare, and military alliance

summaries.value: The princes Philip of France, David of Scotland, and Humphrey of England travel incognito to aid King Alfour of Sicily against a fictionalized Turkish invasion. Their military service, captivity, alliances, concealed identities, and love for Alfour's daughter Iolante lead to Christian victory, royal succession, a tournament for Iolante's hand, dynastic marriages, and conversions. The romance joins chivalric adventure and political counsel to strongly partisan late-medieval Christian representations of Islam and warfare.

The record describes the complete Middle English narrative at Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v, printed by Furnivall on pp. 1–207 and encoded as ThreeKSon:1–45. It excludes the preceding Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV at ff. 1r–7r, Furnivall's forewords and p. 208 note, Thomas Austin's glossary on pp. 209–216, and all digital and physical transmission objects as intellectual parts.

Ward describes forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters indicated by illuminated initials. Furnivall supplied running page glosses, and U-M reused those glosses as DIV1 headings. The chapter boundaries are therefore manuscript-supported while their displayed labels are modern editorial navigation. The repository XML has forty-five DIV1 elements, 115 work-text P elements outside note ancestry, 207 print-page markers across TEXT, and 231 folio-side milestones from 8v through 123v; f. 8r is identified in Furnivall's opening note rather than a milestone.

notes: This is Furnivall's expanded edition title, not evidence that Furnivall translated the work.
titles.value: The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French)

work.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; French source also anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Late fifteenth century; attested by the sole surviving English witness dated c. 1475–c. 1485
work.editor: F. J. Furnivall (1895 source edition), from a working transcript made by Mrs Leon Kellner

publication.embeddedTitle: The Three Kings' Sons. Part I. The Text / edited from its unique MS, Harleian 326, about 1500 A.D. by F.J. Furnivall — The Three Kings' Sons.

work.summary: The princes Philip of France, David of Scotland, and Humphrey of England travel incognito to aid King Alfour of Sicily against a fictionalized Turkish invasion. Their military service, captivity, alliances, concealed identities, and love for Alfour's daughter Iolante lead to Christian victory, royal succession, a tournament for Iolante's hand, dynastic marriages, and conversions. The romance joins chivalric adventure and political counsel to strongly partisan late-medieval Christian representations of Islam and warfare.
